questions.question: Which of the following is the first step recommended for setting up a perfect inbox in Notion Mail?

options.text: Create reusable snippets
options.explanation: Snippets are for saving responses, not the first step in inbox setup.

options.text: Use filters to hide the noise
options.explanation: The guide lists using filters as the first of three steps to set up the inbox.

options.text: Group emails by priority
options.explanation: Grouping comes after filters in the three‑step process.

options.text: Enable the auto‑label button
options.explanation: Auto‑label is a later feature, not the initial setup step.

questions.question: How does Notion AI help with labeling incoming emails?

options.text: It automatically creates new folders for each sender.
options.explanation: AI labels, it doesn't create folders; folders are a traditional email concept.

options.text: It learns your preferences and tags emails based on domains, people, or phrases you specify.
options.explanation: The auto‑label feature lets you tell AI which domains, senders, or keywords to tag.

options.text: It moves all unread messages to a separate view.
options.explanation: Unread filtering is a manual filter, not AI labeling.

options.text: It deletes low‑priority emails automatically.
options.explanation: AI does not delete messages; it only tags them for you.

questions.question: What advantage does the "Group by" option provide compared to simple filters?

options.text: It allows you to hide emails permanently.
options.explanation: Grouping organizes, not hides, emails.

options.text: It lets you structure the inbox into sections such as domain, sender, or priority.
options.explanation: Group by creates visual sections based on chosen criteria, enhancing organization.

options.text: It automatically marks grouped emails as read.
options.explanation: Marking as read is unrelated to grouping.

options.text: It converts emails into Notion pages.
options.explanation: Conversion to pages is a separate feature, not part of grouping.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes a "snippet" in Notion Mail?

options.text: A short video tutorial embedded in an email.
options.explanation: Snippets are text‑based response templates, not videos.

options.text: A saved draft that can be reused for future replies.
options.explanation: Snippets are reusable response blocks you save to insert quickly.

options.text: An AI‑generated calendar event.
options.explanation: Calendar events are created via scheduling, not snippets.

options.text: A label automatically applied to incoming emails.
options.explanation: Labels are applied by auto‑label, not the same as snippets.

questions.question: If you want to quickly set up a view that highlights sales leads, which template should you start from?

options.text: The "priority" template.
options.explanation: The priority template focuses on task‑like grouping, not sales leads.

options.text: The "sales" template.
options.explanation: The guide mentions a pre‑built "sales" template for organizing lead‑related emails.

options.text: The "calendar" template.
options.explanation: Calendar templates relate to scheduling, not email grouping.

options.text: The "snippets" template.
options.explanation: Snippets are for saved responses, not view creation.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the smart scheduling button in Notion Mail?

options.text: To automatically delete conflicting meeting requests.
options.explanation: The button creates scheduling links; it does not delete anything.

options.text: To generate a "Schedule here" link that recipients can use to pick a meeting time.
options.explanation: The button lets you select time blocks and inserts a link for the recipient to schedule.

options.text: To convert an email thread into a Notion page.
options.explanation: Conversion to a page is a separate action, not performed by the scheduling button.

options.text: To automatically add the email sender to your contacts list.
options.explanation: Contact management is unrelated to the scheduling feature.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about custom tags in Notion Mail is FALSE?

options.text: Custom tags can be created using the "select" property.
options.explanation: The guide confirms you can create custom tags via the select property.

options.text: Custom tags are only visible in the "Group by" view and cannot be used with filters.
options.explanation: Tags can be used both for grouping and as filter criteria; the statement limits them incorrectly.

options.text: Tags can be applied automatically by Notion AI when you set up auto‑label preferences.
options.explanation: AI can automatically apply tags based on the preferences you define.

options.text: You can mix custom tags with built‑in filters like "unread" to refine your view.
options.explanation: Mixing custom tags with built‑in filters is a supported workflow.

questions.question: How does the "priority view" transform the inbox experience?

options.text: It hides all emails that are not marked as urgent.
options.explanation: Priority view organizes, not hides, non‑urgent messages.

options.text: It groups emails into a to‑do list based on priority tags, making it act like a task manager.
options.explanation: The guide describes the priority view as turning the inbox into a powerful to‑do list.

options.text: It automatically replies to low‑priority emails with a canned response.
options.explanation: Automatic replies are not part of the priority view functionality.

options.text: It syncs the inbox with external project‑management tools.
options.explanation: Syncing with external tools is not mentioned as a function of the priority view.

questions.question: Which workflow best utilizes Notion AI’s writer when responding to a candidate’s inquiry about application status?

options.text: Manually type the entire response without using any AI suggestions.
options.explanation: The AI writer is intended to speed up and improve responses, not be avoided.

options.text: Select a pre‑written quick‑reply suggestion, customize it, and then save it as a snippet for future use.
options.explanation: This combines AI suggestions, customization, and snippet saving, matching the recommended workflow.

options.text: Use the auto‑label button to tag the email, then ignore the content.
options.explanation: Labeling does not help craft a reply to the candidate.

options.text: Create a new calendar event directly from the email without responding.
options.explanation: Scheduling is unrelated to answering the status inquiry.

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of saving a response as a snippet after adding a scheduling link?

options.text: It automatically updates the recipient’s calendar with the meeting time.
options.explanation: The snippet stores the content; it does not push calendar events.

options.text: It allows you to reuse a fully formatted email—including the scheduling link—without recreating it each time.
options.explanation: Snippets preserve the entire response, making repeated sending efficient.

options.text: It disables the smart scheduling button for future emails.
options.explanation: Saving a snippet does not affect the scheduling button’s functionality.

options.text: It converts the email into a Notion task automatically.
options.explanation: Conversion to a task is a separate action, not a result of saving a snippet.
